 which were a group in korea referred to by the same character[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:59, 16 March 2025 (UTC)}}

{{Otheruses}}

{{Unreferenced|date=March 2015}}
'''Yakko''' (奴) refers to the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} {{ill|Retainer|en|Retainer|WD=Q7316627|lt=Retainer}} class of [[:en:servants|servants]]. In ancient times, as depicted in the [[Kojiki]], the term "yakko" is believed to have referred to the {{ill|slave|en|slave|WD=Q12773225|lt=slave}} class{{Citation needed|date=December 2021}}.

== Characteristics ==
{{Unreferenced section|date=March 2015}}
[[File:Yakko-no-Neri ac (8).jpg|thumb|Yakko Furikomi (Kariya City, Aichi Prefecture)]]

Yakko were of the lowest Rank among {{ill|samurai retainers|ja|武家奉公人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai retainers|qq=}} working in samurai households. They were often disparagingly called "chūgen" ({{ill|Chūgen|ja|中間|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūgen|1=中間|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11367784}}) or "orisuke" ({{ill|Orisuke|ja|折助|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Orisuke|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=奴|1=Yakko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446610}}). The term "yakko" is thought to derive from the ancient {{ill|slave|ja|奴隷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/slave|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=slave|WD=Q12773225}} system's term "yakko" and "yatsuko" (家つ子), meaning "house child." Notably, "yakko" also has parallels in {{ill|Greek|en|Greek|WD=Q227823|lt=Greek}} and {{ill|Latin|en|Latin|WD=Q397|lt=Latin}}, where terms for "household members" also came to mean "slave"{{Sfn|Kitō|1994|page=217}}.

Yakko were often employed from the second or third sons of {{ill|Peasant|en|Peasant|WD=Q838811|lt=farmers}} or common {{ill|Chōnin|en|Chōnin|WD=Q115847|lt=townspeople}} who could not inherit family occupations. Their work included carrying the baggage of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}} on outings and performing various other menial tasks{{Citation needed|date=December 2021}}.

The {{ill|Uniform|en|Uniform|WD=Q7434|lt=uniform}} of yakko, often a {{ill|hanten|ja|半纏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hanten|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=袢纏|1=hanten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1132685}}, bore a large rectangular pattern instead of the family crest of the master. This pattern, called "kuginuki" (釘抜, nail-puller crest), derived from the square shape of a washer. This association led to terms like "yakko-giri" (奴に切る), describing cutting food into larger cube shapes. Cold {{ill|Tofu|en|Tofu|WD=Q177378|lt=tofu}} served in such shapes is called "hiyayakko" (冷奴){{Sfn|Tsuyutani|19pp|loc=「（三十四）奴豆腐」|page=30頁 (コマ番号0017.jp2)}}{{Sfn|Murai|1924|loc=「磯奴豆腐」、「枝豆の奴豆腐」|pages=185-186頁 (コマ番号0pp3.jp2-0pp4.jp2)}}; when heated, it becomes "netsuyakko" or "yuyakko"{{Sfn|Tsuyutani|19pp|loc=「（九十七）湯奴 」|pages=67−68頁 (コマ番号0035.jp2-)}}.

The "yakko kite" ({{ill|Yakko kite|ja|奴凧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakko kite|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=凧|1=kite|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42861}}), commonly seen during New Year kite-flying, mimics a yakko holding the edges of their hanten's sleeves wide open{{Efn|Some {{ill|Ukiyo-e|en|Ukiyo-e|WD=Q185905|lt=ukiyo-e}} from the late Edo period depict people mimicking yakko{{Sfn|Utagawa|1862|loc=「奴凧平（市川市蔵・夢想兵衛 下り中村鶴助）」2枚組の右片}}.}}. The kite's design includes a sleeve-like structure for stability{{Sfn|Akiyama|1986|pages=1362–1365}}.

The term "yakko" also influenced cultural expressions. Male {{ill|performers|ja|芸人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/performers|1=geinin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615883}} and women from {{ill|Yūkaku|en|Yūkaku|WD=Q1060846|lt=brothels}} or {{ill|Hanamachi|lt=geisha districts|ja|花柳界|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanamachi|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=花街|1=hanamachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1574228}} often imitated the behaviors of yakko, leading to terms like "○○yakko" being used for {{ill|courtesans|ja|遊女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/courtesans|1=yūjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q12609698}} or {{ill|geisha|ja|芸者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/geisha|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=芸妓|1=geisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q82723}}, which continue to appear in {{ill|Stage name|en|Stage name|WD=Q1055303|lt=stage names}}.

== Yakko in Media ==
{{See also|historical reenactment parade}}

Yakko costumes appear in {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=kabuki}}, {{ill|Shosagoto|en|Shosagoto|WD=Q11545264|lt=kabuki dance}}, and {{ill|Historical reenactment parade|lt=historical parades|ja|時代行列|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historical reenactment parade|qq=}} {{Efn|During celebrations for the Showa Emperor's enthronement, historical parades featured yakko in daimyō processions{{Sfn|Kyōshinsya|1929|loc=「時代風俗行列」|page= (コマ番号0122.jp2)}} and palanquin bearers{{Sfn|Kyōshinsya|1929|loc=「元氣な奴さん /  大久保彦左衞門」|page= (コマ番号0124.jp2)}}.}}. The costume includes a padded hanten with a "kuginuki" crest, a wide round belt (maruguke), and visible {{ill|fundoshi|ja|褌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fundoshi|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=ふんどし|1=fundoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q976963}}{{Anchors|Fundoshi|Sagari}} to show non-samurai status{{Sfn|Toyokuni|1860|loc=「一世一代岸沢古式部 § 花見帰の供奴」}}. Black or dark-colored {{ill|Tabi|en|Tabi|WD=Q775874|lt=tabi}} are commonly worn{{Sfn|Utagawa|1862|loc=「奴凧平（市川市蔵・夢想兵衛 下り中村鶴助）」2枚組の右片}}. A lively {{ill|端唄|ja|端唄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/端唄|1=hauta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598508}} titled "Yakko-san" captures their spirit{{Sfn|Sakai|1923|page=1|ws=『竹保流[[尺八]]音譜著名端唄全集』}}{{Sfn|Yoshida|1936|page=46|ws=『尺八練習百曲集』}}.

== Yakko in Media ==
{{See also|historical reenactment parade}}

Yakko costumes appear in {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=kabuki|1=Kabuki}}, {{ill|Shosagoto|en|Shosagoto|WD=Q11545264|lt=kabuki dance|1=Shosagoto}}, and {{ill|Historical reenactment parade|lt=historical parades|ja|時代行列|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historical reenactment parade|qq=}} {{Efn|During celebrations for the Showa Emperor's enthronement, historical parades featured yakko in daimyō processions{{Sfn|Kyōshinsya|1929|loc=「時代風俗行列」|page= (コマ番号0122.jp2)}} and palanquin bearers{{Sfn|Kyōshinsya|1929|loc=「元氣な奴さん /  大久保彦左衞門」|page= (コマ番号0124.jp2)}}.}}. The costume includes a padded hanten with a "kuginuki" crest, a wide round belt (maruguke), and visible {{ill|fundoshi|ja|褌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fundoshi|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=ふんどし|1=Fundoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q976963}}{{Anchors|Fundoshi|Sagari}} to show non-samurai status{{Sfn|Toyokuni|1860|loc=「一世一代岸沢古式部 § 花見帰の供奴」}}. Black or dark-colored {{ill|Tabi|en|Tabi|WD=Q775874|lt=tabi|1=Tabi}} are commonly worn{{Sfn|Utagawa|1862|loc=「奴凧平（市川市蔵・夢想兵衛 下り中村鶴助）」2枚組の右片}}. A lively {{ill|端唄|ja|端唄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/端唄|1=hauta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598508}} titled "Yakko-san" captures their spirit{{Sfn|Sakai|1923|page=1|ws=『竹保流[[尺八]]音譜著名端唄全集』}}{{Sfn|Yoshida|1936|page=46|ws=『尺八練習百曲集』}}.

{{Otheruses}}

{{Unreferenced|date=March 2015}}
'''Yakko''' (奴) refers to the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} {{ill|Retainer|en|Retainer|WD=Q7316627|lt=Retainer|1=Retainer}} class of [[:en:servants|servants]]. In ancient times, as depicted in the [[Kojiki]], the term "yakko" is believed to have referred to the {{ill|slave|en|slave|WD=Q12773225|lt=slave|1=Slave}} class{{Citation needed|date=December 2021}}.

== Characteristics ==
{{Unreferenced section|date=March 2015}}
[[File:Yakko-no-Neri ac (8).jpg|thumb|Yakko Furikomi (Kariya City, Aichi Prefecture)]]

Yakko were of the lowest Rank among {{ill|samurai retainers|ja|武家奉公人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai retainers|qq=}} working in samurai households. They were often disparagingly called "chūgen" ({{ill|Chūgen|ja|中間|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chūgen|1=中間|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11367784}}) or "orisuke" ({{ill|Orisuke|ja|折助|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Orisuke|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=奴|1=Yakko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11446610}}). The term "yakko" is thought to derive from the ancient {{ill|slave|ja|奴隷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/slave|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Slave|WD=Q12773225}} system's term "yakko" and "yatsuko" (家つ子), meaning "house child." Notably, "yakko" also has parallels in {{ill|Greek|en|Greek|WD=Q227823|lt=Greek|1=Greek}} and {{ill|Latin|en|Latin|WD=Q397|lt=Latin|1=Latin}}, where terms for "household members" also came to mean "slave"{{Sfn|Kitō|1994|page=217}}.

Yakko were often employed from the second or third sons of {{ill|Peasant|en|Peasant|WD=Q838811|lt=farmers|1=Peasant}} or common {{ill|Chōnin|en|Chōnin|WD=Q115847|lt=townspeople|1=Chōnin}} who could not inherit family occupations. Their work included carrying the baggage of {{ill|samurai|ja|武士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1975935}} on outings and performing various other menial tasks{{Citation needed|date=December 2021}}.

The {{ill|Uniform|en|Uniform|WD=Q7434|lt=uniform|1=Uniform}} of yakko, often a {{ill|hanten|ja|半纏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hanten|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=袢纏|1=Hanten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1132685}}, bore a large rectangular pattern instead of the family crest of the master. This pattern, called "kuginuki" (釘抜, nail-puller crest), derived from the square shape of a washer. This association led to terms like "yakko-giri" (奴に切る), describing cutting food into larger cube shapes. Cold {{ill|Tofu|en|Tofu|WD=Q177378|lt=tofu|1=Tofu}} served in such shapes is called "hiyayakko" (冷奴){{Sfn|Tsuyutani|19pp|loc=「（三十四）奴豆腐」|page=30頁 (コマ番号0017.jp2)}}{{Sfn|Murai|1924|loc=「磯奴豆腐」、「枝豆の奴豆腐」|pages=185-186頁 (コマ番号0pp3.jp2-0pp4.jp2)}}; when heated, it becomes "netsuyakko" or "yuyakko"{{Sfn|Tsuyutani|19pp|loc=「（九十七）湯奴 」|pages=67−68頁 (コマ番号0035.jp2-)}}.

The "yakko kite" ({{ill|Yakko kite|ja|奴凧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yakko kite|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=凧|1=Kite|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q42861}}), commonly seen during New Year kite-flying, mimics a yakko holding the edges of their hanten's sleeves wide open{{Efn|Some {{ill|Ukiyo-e|en|Ukiyo-e|WD=Q185905|lt=ukiyo-e|1=Ukiyo-e}} from the late Edo period depict people mimicking yakko{{Sfn|Utagawa|1862|loc=「奴凧平（市川市蔵・夢想兵衛 下り中村鶴助）」2枚組の右片}}.}}. The kite's design includes a sleeve-like structure for stability{{Sfn|Akiyama|1986|pages=1362–1365}}.

The term "yakko" also influenced cultural expressions. Male {{ill|performers|ja|芸人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/performers|1=geinin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11615883}} and women from {{ill|Yūkaku|en|Yūkaku|WD=Q1060846|lt=brothels|1=Yūkaku}} or {{ill|Hanamachi|lt=geisha districts|ja|花柳界|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hanamachi|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=花街|1=Hanamachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1574228}} often imitated the behaviors of yakko, leading to terms like "○○yakko" being used for {{ill|courtesans|ja|遊女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/courtesans|1=yūjo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q12609698}} or {{ill|geisha|ja|芸者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/geisha|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=芸妓|1=Geisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q82723}}, which continue to appear in {{ill|Stage name|en|Stage name|WD=Q1055303|lt=stage names|1=Stage name}}.

== Yakko in Media ==
{{See also|historical reenactment parade}}

Yakko costumes appear in {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=kabuki|1=Kabuki}}, {{ill|Shosagoto|en|Shosagoto|WD=Q11545264|lt=kabuki dance|1=Shosagoto}}, and {{ill|Historical reenactment parade|lt=historical parades|ja|時代行列|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historical reenactment parade|qq=}} {{Efn|During celebrations for the Showa Emperor's enthronement, historical parades featured yakko in daimyō processions{{Sfn|Kyōshinsya|1929|loc=「時代風俗行列」|page= (コマ番号0122.jp2)}} and palanquin bearers{{Sfn|Kyōshinsya|1929|loc=「元氣な奴さん /  大久保彦左衞門」|page= (コマ番号0124.jp2)}}.}}. The costume includes a padded hanten with a "kuginuki" crest, a wide round belt (maruguke), and visible {{ill|fundoshi|ja|褌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fundoshi|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=ふんどし|1=Fundoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q976963}}{{Anchors|Fundoshi|Sagari}} to show non-samurai status{{Sfn|Toyokuni|1860|loc=「一世一代岸沢古式部 § 花見帰の供奴」}}. Black or dark-colored {{ill|Tabi|en|Tabi|WD=Q775874|lt=tabi|1=Tabi}} are commonly worn{{Sfn|Utagawa|1862|loc=「奴凧平（市川市蔵・夢想兵衛 下り中村鶴助）」2枚組の右片}}. A lively {{ill|端唄|ja|端唄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/端唄|1=hauta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598508}} titled "Yakko-san" captures their spirit{{Sfn|Sakai|1923|page=1|ws=『竹保流[[尺八]]音譜著名端唄全集』}}{{Sfn|Yoshida|1936|page=46|ws=『尺八練習百曲集』}}.

== Yakko in Media ==
{{See also|historical reenactment parade}}

Yakko costumes appear in {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=kabuki|1=Kabuki}}, {{ill|Shosagoto|en|Shosagoto|WD=Q11545264|lt=kabuki dance|1=Shosagoto}}, and {{ill|Historical reenactment parade|lt=historical parades|ja|時代行列|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Historical reenactment parade|qq=}} {{Efn|During celebrations for the Showa Emperor's enthronement, historical parades featured yakko in daimyō processions{{Sfn|Kyōshinsya|1929|loc=「時代風俗行列」|page= (コマ番号0122.jp2)}} and palanquin bearers{{Sfn|Kyōshinsya|1929|loc=「元氣な奴さん /  大久保彦左衞門」|page= (コマ番号0124.jp2)}}.}}. The costume includes a padded hanten with a "kuginuki" crest, a wide round belt (maruguke), and visible {{ill|fundoshi|ja|褌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/fundoshi|ja_comment=fixed jawiki redirect|3=ふんどし|1=Fundoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q976963}}{{Anchors|Fundoshi|Sagari}} to show non-samurai status{{Sfn|Toyokuni|1860|loc=「一世一代岸沢古式部 § 花見帰の供奴」}}. Black or dark-colored {{ill|Tabi|en|Tabi|WD=Q775874|lt=tabi|1=Tabi}} are commonly worn{{Sfn|Utagawa|1862|loc=「奴凧平（市川市蔵・夢想兵衛 下り中村鶴助）」2枚組の右片}}. A lively {{ill|端唄|ja|端唄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/端唄|1=hauta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11598508}} titled "Yakko-san" captures their spirit{{Sfn|Sakai|1923|page=1|ws=『竹保流[[尺八]]音譜著名端唄全集』}}{{Sfn|Yoshida|1936|page=46|ws=『尺八練習百曲集』}}.
